questions.title: Contains Duplicate
questions.concept_refresher: Use a Hash Set to store elements as you iterate through the array.
questions.mental_model: You scan the array once; for each number you ask 'have I already seen this in my set?'. If yes, you immediately know there is a duplicate.

questions.title: Valid Anagram
questions.concept_refresher: Count the frequency of each character using a Hash Map or an array of size 26.
questions.mental_model: Think of each word as a histogram of letters. Two strings are anagrams if their histograms are identical, so you increment counts for s and decrement for t and verify all counts end at zero.

questions.title: Two Sum
questions.concept_refresher: Use a Hash Map from value to index while iterating through the array.
questions.mental_model: For each number x you compute target - x and ask 'did I already see this complement?'. The hashmap lets you find complements in O(1), so the first time both sides of the sum appear you can return their indices.

questions.title: Group Anagrams
questions.concept_refresher: Use a Hash Map where the key is either the sorted string or a 26-length frequency signature and the value is a list of words.
questions.mental_model: Every anagram family shares the same 'fingerprint' (sorted letters or frequency vector). You turn each word into its fingerprint and drop it into the matching bucket in the hashmap.

questions.title: Top K Frequent Elements
questions.concept_refresher: First build a frequency Hash Map, then either use bucket sort (array of lists indexed by frequency) or a heap to extract the k most frequent.
questions.mental_model: Picture buckets where index = frequency. You throw each value into the bucket of its count, then scan buckets from highest frequency downward, collecting elements until you have k.

questions.title: Product of Array Except Self
questions.concept_refresher: Compute prefix products (product of all elements to the left) and postfix products (to the right) without using division.
questions.mental_model: For index i, the answer is 'product of everything on the left' × 'product of everything on the right'. You can get both in O(n) using two passes while reusing one result array.

questions.title: Valid Sudoku
questions.concept_refresher: Track seen digits using sets (or bitmasks) for each row, each column, and each 3×3 sub-box.
questions.mental_model: Treat each placement as a constraint check: before placing digit d in (r, c), you ask 'is d already in row r, column c, or box b?'. If it appears in any of the three sets, the board is invalid.

questions.title: Encode and Decode Strings
questions.concept_refresher: Use length‑prefixed encoding: for each string write '<length>#<string>' concatenated together.
questions.mental_model: The decoder walks the string, first reading digits until '#', interpreting them as the length L, then taking exactly L characters as the next word. Length metadata makes special characters inside the words irrelevant.

questions.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
questions.concept_refresher: Insert all numbers into a Hash Set, and only start counting from numbers that have no predecessor (n − 1 not in the set).
questions.mental_model: Think of each consecutive run as starting at a 'head' value with no left neighbor. For each head, you walk right (n, n+1, n+2, …) as long as numbers exist in the set, tracking the longest run length.

questions.title: Valid Palindrome
questions.concept_refresher: Normalize the string (ignore non‑alphanumeric, lower‑case), then use two pointers from both ends moving inward.
questions.mental_model: Imagine two fingers, one at the start and one at the end of the cleaned string. They walk toward each other and must always see the same character; a mismatch means it's not a palindrome.

questions.title: 3Sum
questions.concept_refresher: Sort the array, then for each fixed index i use two pointers (l, r) to find triplets that sum to zero while skipping duplicates.
questions.mental_model: Think of anchoring one number and then squeezing a window [l, r] around it. If the sum is too big you shrink from the right, if too small you grow from the left, and only exact zero gets recorded.

questions.title: Container With Most Water
questions.concept_refresher: Use two pointers at the ends, compute area each time, and move the pointer at the shorter height inward.
questions.mental_model: Water amount is (width) × min(heightLeft, heightRight). Since width shrinks as pointers move, the only chance to improve area is by increasing the limiting (shorter) height.

questions.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
questions.concept_refresher: Track the minimum price seen so far and the best profit if you sold today.
questions.mental_model: As you scan days from left to right, you keep the best 'buy so far'. For each price, you ask 'what if I had bought at the lowest so far and sold today?' and keep the maximum profit.

questions.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
questions.concept_refresher: Use a sliding window with a Hash Set or map to ensure all characters in the window are unique.
questions.mental_model: Think of a stretchy window on the string. You extend the right side until you hit a duplicate, then shrink from the left until the window becomes 'all unique' again, tracking the maximum window size.

questions.title: Permutation in String
questions.concept_refresher: Maintain character frequency counts for s1 and for a fixed-size sliding window over s2.
questions.mental_model: You slide a window of length |s1| over s2. If at some position the character frequency vector of the window equals that of s1, that window is a permutation of s1.

questions.title: Valid Parentheses
questions.concept_refresher: Use a stack; push opening brackets and on each closing bracket check the top of the stack for the matching type.
questions.mental_model: Think of the stack as a 'waiting room' for opens. Every close must perfectly match the most recent open; if at the end the room is empty and no mismatch occurred, the string is valid.

questions.title: Min Stack
questions.concept_refresher: Maintain two stacks: one for values and one for the minimum value at each depth.
questions.mental_model: Each time you push, you also push the new 'current minimum so far'. That way the top of the min‑stack always mirrors the minimum of all elements in the main stack, so getMin is O(1).

questions.title: Binary Search
questions.concept_refresher: Use two indices (left, right), compute mid, and shrink the search range depending on comparison with target.
questions.mental_model: You repeatedly ask 'is the target in the left half or the right half?'. Each step throws away half of the remaining array, giving O(log n) time.

questions.title: Invert Binary Tree
questions.concept_refresher: Use DFS or BFS and swap left and right children at every node.
questions.mental_model: Imagine holding a mirror in front of the tree. Recursively, each node swaps its left and right pointers, so the structure becomes the mirror image of the original.

questions.title: Merge Intervals
questions.concept_refresher: Sort intervals by start time, then merge by comparing each interval to the last one in the output.
questions.mental_model: Once intervals are sorted, you maintain a 'current merged interval'. If the next interval overlaps, you extend the end; if it does not, you close the current one and start a new merged block.

questions.title: Subsets
questions.concept_refresher: Use backtracking: at each index decide to either include the element in the current subset or skip it.
questions.mental_model: You explore a binary decision tree where each level answers 'take this element or not?'. Each path from root to leaf corresponds to one subset.
